theme: Pack wireless links against additive interference
title: Additive Interference Spectrum Packing
seed_problem: link scheduling under a channel-conflict graph (colour links so no two conflicting links share a channel)
mutation: composite: replaces the pairwise conflict-graph feasibility test with a genuine additive-SINR feasibility test (a receiver's interference is the SUM of every co-channel transmitter's power it hears, not just its worst single neighbour), and couples channel assignment to a per-link DISCRETE POWER choice, so an accepted link can trade power (and therefore the interference it imposes on others) for spatial reuse -- something no graph colouring can express.
innovation_hook: Feasibility is a cumulative-interference constraint, not a pairwise conflict, so power-controlled spatial reuse beats any graph-coloring assignment.
strategies: trivial: no spatial reuse at all -- give the min(N,C) highest-value links one link per channel at max power (reproduces the checker's baseline, ratio ~0.1)

naive greedy: build a PAIRWISE conflict graph (max power, one interferer at a time) and colour it with C colours, turning every link ON at max power; blind to additive interference, so dense clusters of individually-mild co-channel interferers collectively drown each other

insight (strong): admit links value-first against the TRUE cumulative SINR, using the MINIMUM power level that still clears threshold for each accepted link so it imposes the least possible interference on channel-mates -- trading power for reuse to pack far more value per channel than any coloring

a general solver can go further with local search: swap channels/power levels of already-accepted links, or re-order admission by value-density under the current interference state, to escape the greedy admission order's local optima
